section.title: The Raising of Lazarus
section.sectionRationale: The seventh and greatest sign brings the Book of Signs to its climax. Jesus deliberately delays, lets His friend Lazarus die, then raises him after four days in the tomb, declaring, I am the resurrection and the life. The sign both reveals Jesus as the conqueror of death and, ironically, seals His own death, as the Sanhedrin resolves to kill Him, and Caiaphas unwittingly prophesies that one man must die for the people.
section.thesis: Jesus, who is Himself the resurrection and the life, weeps at the grave of His friend and then calls him out of the tomb after four days, revealing the glory of God and giving a sign that He is the Christ who conquers death, even as the raising of Lazarus sets in motion the plot that will bring Jesus to His own death for the people.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the news of Lazarus's sickness and Jesus' deliberate delay; the meeting with Martha and the great claim, I am the resurrection and the life; Jesus weeping with Mary at the grave; the raising of Lazarus from the tomb; and the Sanhedrin's plot and Caiaphas's unwitting prophecy.

units.label: The death of Lazarus
units.note: this sickness is for the glory of God

verses.text: At this time a man named Lazarus was sick. He lived in Bethany, the village of Mary and her sister Martha.
(Mary, whose brother Lazarus was sick, was to anoint the Lord with perfume and wipe His feet with her hair.)
So the sisters sent word to Jesus, “Lord, the one You love is sick.”
When Jesus heard this, He said, “This sickness will not end in death. No, it is for the glory of God, so that the Son of God may be glorified through it.”
Now Jesus loved Martha and her sister and Lazarus.
So on hearing that Lazarus was sick, He stayed where He was for two days,
and then He said to the disciples, “Let us go back to Judea.”
“Rabbi,” they replied, “the Jews just tried to stone You, and You are going back there?”
Jesus answered, “Are there not twelve hours of daylight? If anyone walks in the daytime, he will not stumble, because he sees by the light of this world.
But if anyone walks at night, he will stumble, because he has no light.”
After He had said this, He told them, “Our friend Lazarus has fallen asleep, but I am going there to wake him up.”
His disciples replied, “Lord, if he is sleeping, he will get better.”
They thought that Jesus was talking about actual sleep, but He was speaking about the death of Lazarus.
So Jesus told them plainly, “Lazarus is dead,
and for your sake I am glad I was not there, so that you may believe. But let us go to him.”
Then Thomas called Didymus said to his fellow disciples, “Let us also go, so that we may die with Him.”

units.label: I am the resurrection and the life

verses.text: When Jesus arrived, He found that Lazarus had already spent four days in the tomb.
Now Bethany was near Jerusalem, a little less than two miles away,
and many of the Jews had come to Martha and Mary to console them in the loss of their brother.
So when Martha heard that Jesus was coming, she went out to meet Him, but Mary stayed at home.
Martha said to Jesus, “Lord, if You had been here, my brother would not have died.
But even now I know that God will give You whatever You ask of Him.”
“Your brother will rise again,” Jesus told her.
Martha replied, “I know that he will rise again in the resurrection at the last day.”
Jesus said to her, “{{egoeimi|I am}} the {{anastasis|resurrection}} and the {{zoe|life}}. Whoever believes in Me will live, even though he dies.
And everyone who lives and believes in Me will never die. Do you believe this?”
“Yes, Lord,” she answered, “I believe that You are the Christ, the Son of God, who was to come into the world.”
After Martha had said this, she went back and called her sister Mary aside to tell her, “The Teacher is here and is asking for you.”
And when Mary heard this, she got up quickly and went to Him.
Now Jesus had not yet entered the village, but was still at the place where Martha had met Him.
When the Jews who were in the house consoling Mary saw how quickly she got up and went out, they followed her, supposing she was going to the tomb to mourn there.
When Mary came to Jesus and saw Him, she fell at His feet and said, “Lord, if You had been here, my brother would not have died.”
When Jesus saw her weeping, and the Jews who had come with her also weeping, He was deeply moved in spirit and troubled.
“Where have you put him?” He asked. “Come and see, Lord,” they answered.
Jesus wept.
Then the Jews said, “See how He loved him!”
But some of them asked, “Could not this man who opened the eyes of the blind also have kept Lazarus from dying?”
Jesus, once again deeply moved, came to the tomb. It was a cave with a stone laid across the entrance.
“Take away the stone,” Jesus said. “Lord, by now he stinks,” said Martha, the sister of the dead man. “It has already been four days.”
Jesus replied, “Did I not tell you that if you believed, you would see the {{doxa|glory}} of God?”
So they took away the stone. Then Jesus lifted His eyes upward and said, “Father, I thank You that You have heard Me.
I knew that You always hear Me, but I say this for the benefit of the people standing here, so they may believe that You sent Me.”
After Jesus had said this, He called out in a loud voice, “Lazarus, come out!”
The man who had been dead came out with his hands and feet bound in strips of linen, and his face wrapped in a cloth. “Unwrap him and let him go,” Jesus told them.

units.label: The plot to kill Jesus
units.note: one man must die for the people

verses.text: Therefore many of the Jews who had come to Mary, and had seen what Jesus did, believed in Him.
But some of them went to the Pharisees and told them what Jesus had done.
Then the chief priests and Pharisees convened the Sanhedrin and said, “What are we to do? This man is performing many signs.
If we let Him go on like this, everyone will believe in Him, and then the Romans will come and take away both our place and our nation.”
But one of them, named Caiaphas, who was high priest that year, said to them, “You know nothing at all!
You do not realize that it is better for you that one man die for the people than that the whole nation perish.”
Caiaphas did not say this on his own. Instead, as high priest that year, he was prophesying that Jesus would die for the nation,
and not only for the nation, but also for the scattered children of God, to gather them together into one.
So from that day on they plotted to kill Him.
As a result, Jesus no longer went about publicly among the Jews, but He withdrew to a town called Ephraim in an area near the wilderness. And He stayed there with the disciples.
Now the Jewish Passover was near, and many people went up from the country to Jerusalem to purify themselves before the Passover.
They kept looking for Jesus and asking one another as they stood in the temple courts, “What do you think? Will He come to the feast at all?”
But the chief priests and Pharisees had given orders that anyone who knew where He was must report it, so that they could arrest Him.

egoeimi.gloss: I am. In the fifth great I am saying, Jesus tells Martha, I am the resurrection and the life. He does not merely promise resurrection at the last day; He is Himself resurrection and life in person, so that the future hope becomes present in Him. To be joined to Him by faith is to have life that death cannot end.

anastasis.gloss: Resurrection, a rising up from the dead. Martha believed in a resurrection at the last day; Jesus reveals that the resurrection is not only an event but a person, Himself. The raising of Lazarus is a foretaste and pledge, a sign pointing to Jesus' own resurrection and to the final rising of all who are His.

zoe.gloss: Life. Jesus is the life, the source of the very life of God, so that whoever believes in Him will live even though he dies, and whoever lives and believes will never finally die. The word gathers up John's insistence that eternal life is found in the Son and cannot be undone by the grave.

doxa.gloss: Glory, the revealed splendor of God. Jesus says the sickness is for the glory of God and promises Martha she will see the glory of God if she believes. The raising of Lazarus displays that glory, and in John it points beyond itself to the greater glory of the cross and resurrection, the hour toward which this sign hastens Jesus.

basic.context: Lazarus, whom Jesus loved, falls sick, and his sisters send word. Strangely, Jesus stays where He is two more days, and Lazarus dies. When Jesus finally comes, Lazarus has been in the tomb four days. Martha meets Him with grief and faith, and Jesus tells her, I am the resurrection and the life. At the grave, surrounded by weeping, Jesus Himself weeps, then commands the stone removed and calls, Lazarus, come out, and the dead man walks out still wrapped in grave clothes. Many believe, but the leaders, alarmed, resolve to kill Jesus, and Caiaphas says, without knowing its full truth, that one man should die for the people.
basic.hermeneutics: The delay is deliberate, not neglect; Jesus loves the family and waits so that a greater glory may be revealed. The key claim is I am the resurrection and the life, which lifts resurrection from a distant event to a present person. Jesus' tears show that His power over death does not make Him cold to grief. And notice the deep irony: the sign that gives life to Lazarus is the very thing that seals Jesus' death, and Caiaphas's cynical politics becomes an unwitting prophecy of the atonement.
basic.originalLanguages: The heart of the chapter is the claim ego eimi, I am the anastasis, the resurrection, and the zoe, the life. Jesus is not only the giver of resurrection life but its source in person. The sickness and the sign are for the doxa, the glory, of God, a glory that points ahead to the cross.
basic.typology: Lazarus called from the tomb is a sign and pledge of Jesus' own resurrection and of the final resurrection of all His people. The high priest's word that one must die for the people points to the true High Priest who dies to gather God's scattered children into one.
basic.christ: Christ is the resurrection and the life, who conquers death not merely by a miracle but in His own person. He weeps with the grieving and yet commands the grave. His raising of Lazarus prefigures His own rising, and it drives Him toward the cross where He will die for the people and for the scattered children of God.
basic.god: God is glorified in the Son, hears the Son always, and works even the enemies' plots into His saving purpose. The Father who sent the Son is displaying His glory in the defeat of death and preparing the greater glory of the cross.
basic.crossReferences: Ezekiel 37 pictures God raising the dead to life. John 5 promised the hour when the dead hear the Son's voice. 1 Corinthians 15 proclaims Christ the firstfruits of resurrection. John 12 shows Lazarus's raising drawing crowds to Jesus.
basic.oneStory: Death, the last enemy since the fall, meets its master. Jesus, the resurrection and the life, calls a four-days-dead man from the tomb as a sign of His own coming victory. And the very sign that displays His power over death sets in motion His death for the people, through which the scattered children of God will be gathered into one.

context.literary: The seventh and climactic sign of the Book of Signs, told at length with rising emotional intensity: the delay, the dialogues with Martha and Mary, the tears, the raising, and the fateful council. It is the hinge between the public ministry and the Passion, for the sign of life becomes the trigger of Jesus' death. The fifth I am saying stands at its center, and Caiaphas's prophecy interprets the coming cross.
context.historical: Bethany lay less than two miles from Jerusalem, so the raising happened near the seat of power and drew official attention. Four days in the tomb was significant; a popular belief held that the soul lingered near the body for three days, so four days marked death as beyond any doubt. The Sanhedrin's fear that Rome would remove their place and nation reflects the fragile political situation under Roman rule, and the high priest Caiaphas served during those years.
context.canonical: The raising fulfills the hope of resurrection glimpsed in Ezekiel 37 and Daniel 12 and enacts the promise of John 5:25-29 that the dead will hear the Son's voice. It anticipates 1 Corinthians 15, where Christ is the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep. Caiaphas's unwitting prophecy that one man should die for the people, and for the scattered children of God gathered into one, frames the cross in terms of substitution and the ingathering of Jew and Gentile, John 10:16 and Ephesians 2:14-16.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the signs to their height by revealing Jesus as the resurrection and the life who conquers death, to call for faith in Him in the face of the grave, and to show how the giving of life to Lazarus leads to Jesus' own death for the people, interpreted by Caiaphas's prophecy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a unique miracle, not a promise that every believer's loved one will be raised now. Its abiding teaching is that Jesus is the resurrection and the life, that faith in Him gives life that death cannot end, and that His grief and His power belong together; the call, do you believe this, is put to every reader.

debates.question: Why did Jesus deliberately delay and let Lazarus die?

views.label: For a greater revelation of glory and faith
views.case: Jesus says plainly it is for the glory of God and so that the disciples may believe; had He healed the sickness, there would have been no raising, so the delay serves a fuller display of His power over death and a deeper faith.

views.label: To reveal Himself as resurrection, not merely healer
views.case: By waiting until death was beyond dispute, Jesus moves the encounter from healing to resurrection, unveiling that He is the resurrection and the life in person, not only one who can prevent death.

debates.bearing: Both show that the delay is love, not indifference; Jesus withholds a lesser mercy to give a greater one, and His timing serves the glory of God and the faith of His people.

debates.question: What is the meaning of Caiaphas's prophecy that one man should die for the people?

views.label: Cynical politics used by God
views.case: Caiaphas meant only that sacrificing Jesus would protect the nation from Roman reprisal; John notes that God made his self-serving calculation an unwitting prophecy of the atonement.

views.label: A true statement of substitution and ingathering
views.case: Despite his intent, the words state the gospel: Jesus dies for the nation and for the scattered children of God to gather them into one, a substitutionary death with a worldwide scope.

debates.bearing: Together they show God's sovereignty over even His enemies' words: what Caiaphas meant as cold expediency, God meant as the announcement of Christ's saving death for His people, Jew and Gentile alike.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is anchored in the fifth I am saying, ego eimi, I am the anastasis and the zoe, the resurrection and the life. Jesus does not merely promise a resurrection at the last day, as Martha believed; He declares that resurrection and life are found in His own person, so that whoever is joined to Him by faith lives even though he dies and will never finally die. The whole episode, from the sickness to the raising, is for the doxa, the glory of God, which Martha is told she will see if she believes. That glory, revealed in the raising of Lazarus, points beyond itself to the greater glory of the cross and the empty tomb toward which this sign hastens Jesus.

typology.fulfillment: A sign and pledge of Jesus' own resurrection and of the final resurrection of all who are His, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23.

typology.type: The valley of dry bones raised to life, Ezekiel 37
typology.fulfillment: The Son who calls the dead to life by His voice, John 5:25 and 11:43.

typology.type: One man dying for the people, spoken by the high priest
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true High Priest, dying to gather the scattered children of God into one, John 11:51-52.

study.christ: Christ is revealed as the resurrection and the life, conquering death not by a technique but in His own person, so that life and rising belong to Him and He gives them to all who believe. He is fully human, weeping real tears at the grave of His friend, and fully divine, commanding the dead to come out. The greatest of His signs displays the glory of God and points ahead to His own resurrection. And in the same stroke it drives Him toward the cross, where, as Caiaphas unknowingly prophesied, one man will die for the people and for the scattered children of God, to gather them into one.
study.god: God is glorified in the Son and hears the Son always, as Jesus' prayer at the tomb declares. The Father's glory is displayed in the overthrow of death, the enemy that entered the world at the fall. And God is sovereign even over the plots of His enemies: the council's decision to kill Jesus and the high priest's cynical calculation become the means and the announcement of the saving death God had purposed. Behind the tears, the tomb, and the plot stands the Father whose glory and love are moving the story toward the cross and the empty grave.

crossReferences.note: The hour is coming when the dead will hear the voice of the Son of God and live, enacted in the raising of Lazarus.
God promises to open the graves and bring His people up, a picture fulfilled in Christ the resurrection and the life.
Christ the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep, the resurrection Lazarus's raising foreshadows.
Christ makes the divided into one, the gathering of the scattered children of God Caiaphas unwittingly foretold.

study.oneStory: Death, the last enemy loosed on the world at the fall, meets its master. Jesus, who is Himself the resurrection and the life, calls a man four days dead out of the tomb as a sign of the greater victory to come. He weeps with the grieving even as He conquers the grave, showing a God who is near in sorrow and mighty over death. And in a deep irony that runs through the whole story, the very sign that gives life to Lazarus seals Jesus' own death, through which, as the high priest unknowingly prophesied, He will die for the people and gather the scattered children of God into one.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus stay two more days before going to Lazarus?

options.text: So that God would be glorified and the disciples would believe, through a greater sign than healing.
options.note: Verses 4 and 15 give the purpose: the glory of God and the disciples' faith.

options.text: Because He did not really care about the family.
options.note: Verse 5 says He loved them; the delay was purposeful love, not indifference.

options.text: Because He was afraid to return to Judea.
options.note: The disciples raised the danger, but Jesus chose to go; fear was not His reason for waiting.

options.text: So that a greater miracle of resurrection could reveal who He is.
options.note: True, but it omits the stated aims, the glory of God and the disciples' belief.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by I am the resurrection and the life?

options.text: Resurrection and life are found in His own person, so those who believe in Him live even though they die.
options.note: Verses 25 and 26 make Jesus Himself the source of resurrection life, not just a future event.

options.text: He will one day raise the dead at the last day and nothing more.
options.note: Martha already believed in a last-day resurrection; Jesus reveals He is the resurrection now, in person.

options.text: He promises a good afterlife to those who are moral.
options.note: The promise is life through faith in Him, not a reward for morality.

options.text: Those who trust Him receive life that death cannot end.
options.note: True, but it does not capture the striking claim that He Himself is the resurrection and the life.

quiz.q: What is significant about Jesus weeping at the tomb?

options.text: His power over death does not make Him cold; He enters fully into human grief.
options.note: Verse 35 shows the compassion of the one who is about to raise the dead.

options.text: He wept because He was powerless to help.
options.note: He was about to raise Lazarus; His tears were grief, not helplessness.

options.text: He wept chiefly so the crowd would see His love.
options.note: The onlookers did say see how He loved him, but His tears were genuine grief, not a performance.

options.text: He shared in the sorrow of those who mourned.
options.note: True, but it understates the striking union of real grief with the power that was about to conquer death.

quiz.q: What was the result of the raising of Lazarus among the leaders?

options.text: They convened the Sanhedrin and resolved to kill Jesus.
options.note: Verses 47 to 53 record the plot that followed the sign.

options.text: They repented and believed in Him.
options.note: Many of the people believed, but the leaders plotted His death.

options.text: They acknowledged the sign but refused to believe.
options.note: They could not deny the sign, yet rather than believe they resolved to kill Him.

options.text: They feared the people would follow Him and Rome would act.
options.note: True of their reasoning, but it omits the decisive outcome, that they plotted to kill Him.

quiz.q: What deeper truth lay in Caiaphas's words that one man should die for the people?

options.text: Though he meant cold politics, he unwittingly prophesied that Jesus would die for the nation and gather the scattered children of God into one.
options.note: Verses 51 and 52 reveal the prophecy hidden in his cynical statement.

options.text: He sincerely understood and proclaimed the gospel of the atonement.
options.note: He did not understand; John says God made his self-serving words an unwitting prophecy.

options.text: He was simply making a practical political calculation with no deeper meaning.
options.note: John explicitly says he was prophesying, though he did not grasp it.

options.text: He rightly stated that Jesus' death would benefit the people.
options.note: True in the words themselves, but it misses that this was an unwitting prophecy pointing to the atonement for Jew and Gentile.

questions: Jesus delayed out of love, to give a greater gift. When God seems slow to answer, how might His delay be working a deeper good than the quick help you wanted?
Jesus asked Martha, do you believe this, right at the graveside. How would you answer that question in the face of your own losses?
Jesus wept even though He knew He would raise Lazarus. How does it comfort you that Christ enters your grief rather than brushing it aside?
The sign that gave Lazarus life led Jesus toward His own death. How does it deepen your worship to see that your life came at the cost of His?
Jesus called a dead man by name, and he came out. Where do you need to hear the life-giving voice of Christ calling you out of deadness today?

translationNotes.note: A little less than two miles renders the Greek measure; the BSB footnote gives about fifteen stadia, roughly 1.72 miles. Bethany's nearness to Jerusalem explains the official attention the sign drew.
Deeply moved in spirit; the BSB footnote notes it can be rendered He was indignant in spirit, also in verse 38. Jesus' emotion mingles grief with holy anger at death itself.
Four days in the tomb was understood to place death beyond any doubt, heightening the wonder of the raising.
John explains that Caiaphas, as high priest, unwittingly prophesied Jesus' substitutionary death for the nation and for the scattered children of God, gathered into one.

Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
